---
title: "Causal sufficient dimension reduction for multiple continuous exposures"
subtitle: "an application to environmental mixtures analysis"
author: "Thomas Hsiao, Ph.D. \\newline March 19th, 2026"
date: ""
institute: "Emory University \\newline Rollins School of Public Health \\newline Department of Biostatistics and Bioinformatics"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  beamer:
    classoption: ["4pt"]
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{multicol}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
    incremental: false
    theme: "metropolis"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
    header-includes: |
      % Define custom colors
      \definecolor{customblue}{HTML}{1e376d}
      \definecolor{customgold}{HTML}{d9ab28}

      % Change main color for frame title bar (spanning the slide length)
      \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=customblue,fg=white}

      % Change title text on the title frame to customblue
      \setbeamercolor{title}{bg=customblue,fg=customblue}

      % Modify the color of the horizontal line in the title slide (Metropolis default line)
      \setbeamercolor{title separator}{fg=customgold}

      % Set the section title page bar to customblue
      \setbeamercolor{section title}{fg=customblue,bg=customblue}
      \setbeamercolor{background canvas}{bg=white}

      <!-- % Theorem block -->
      <!-- \setbeamercolor{block title}{bg=customblue,fg=white} -->
      <!-- \setbeamercolor{block body}{bg=lightgray,fg=black} -->

      % Add logo to the top right of the title slide
      \titlegraphic{%
        \hfill%
        \includegraphics[width=3cm]{ShieldEmory_hz_280_pres2023.png}%
      }
      
      % Define the last slide to count in the total (Change this manually to match the "Conclusion" slide number)
      \newcounter{finalslide}
      \setcounter{finalslide}{44} % Change this to the last slide before "Conclusion"
      
      % Add slide number in the lower right
       \setbeamertemplate{footline}{
         \leavevmode%
         \ifnum\value{framenumber}>1
           \ifnum\value{framenumber}>\numexpr\value{finalslide}+1\relax % Hide numbering one slide after "Conclusion"
           \else
             \hfill%
             \usebeamercolor[fg]{page number in head/foot}%
             \usebeamerfont{page number in head/foot}%
             \the\numexpr\value{framenumber}-1\relax{} / \the\value{finalslide}\kern1em
           \fi
         \fi
      }
---

# Introduction

## Goals of Mixtures Analysis

Evaluating the health effects of complex environmental mixtures has become a central priority for the NIEHS. 

. . .

- Assess the joint health impact 

- Identify important contributors

- Inform policy regulations 

## Environmental mixtures analysis methods

Single-index methods ($d=1$):

- Weighted quantile sum regression (WQS) 

- Quantile $g$-computation (qgcomp) 

Dimension reduction methods ($1<d<p$):

- Principal components pursuit regression (PCP) 

- Bayesian multiple index models (BMIM) 

Full exposure-response surface (ERS) methods($d=p$):

- Bayesian kernel machine regression (BKMR) 

- Bayesian additive regression trees (BART) 

- Distributed lag models with regression trees (DLM tree)

## Limitations of existing methods

General structure of the methods is:
$$
Y = h(X) + C^\top \gamma + \epsilon
$$
and differ by how they estimate $h$. 

. . .

1. The truth might lie between the single index and full ERS paradigms.

. . .

2. Many dimension reduction methods don't take into account the outcome $Y$

. . .

3. None of these (except for qgcomp sometimes) are *causal inference* methods.

<!--
## Dimension reduction as a solution

Why I think dimension reduction is the answer:

- Dimension reduction balances **flexibility** and **interpretability** by estimating the *intrinsic dimension* of the ERS.

. . .

- **Single-index models** and **full ERS models** are special cases. 

. . .

- Correlated exposures can be captured through shared latent components.
-->


## Proposed solution

We propose a method, **causal sufficient dimension reduction**, that is simultaneously *causal*, *flexible*, and *interpretable*.

# Causal inference

## Notation

-   Observed data: $O=(Y, X, C) \in \mathcal{Y}\times\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{C}$

    -   $Y \in \mathbb{R}$: continuous outcome\
    -   $X \in \mathbb{R}^p$: continuous exposures\
    -   $Z \in \mathbb{R}^d$: latent exposures \
    -   $C \in \mathbb{R}^q$: covariates

-   Data: i.i.d. sample $({O}_1,\dots,{O}_n)$

-   Potential outcomes: $Y^{x}$ = outcome under exposure $x$

-   Causal mean with intervention $x$: $\mu(x) := \mathbb{E}\!\left[Y^{{x}}\right]$

-   Outcome regression: $m({x}, {c}):= \mathbb{E}(Y \mid {X} = {x}, {C}={c})$

-   Propensity score: $\pi({x}\mid {c})=\partial P({X}\leq {x}\mid {C}={c}) / \partial{x}$

<!--
## Identification of the Causal Mean

**Regression Adjustment Formula**: 
$$
  \mu(x) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ m(x, C) \right]
$$

**Generalized Propensity Score (GPS)**:
$$
  \mu(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \mathbb{E} \left[\frac{Y \cdot K\left(\frac{X-x}{h}\right)}{h^p \cdot \pi(X \mid C)}  \right]
$$
-->

## Doubly robust estimation of causal ERS

Colangelo & Lee (2025) and Zhang & Chen (2025) provide a $\sqrt{nh}$-convergent doubly-robust estimator for the dose-response curve for univariate continuous treatments. 

We extend to the multivariate case as follows:

::: {.callout-important icon=false title="Doubly-robust estimator of $\mu(x)$"}
$$
    \hat{\mu}_{DR}(x) = \frac{1}{nh^p} \sum_{i=1}^n \Bigg\{\frac{K\left(\frac{X_i - x}{h}\right)}{\widehat{\pi}(X_i \mid C_i)}\cdot [Y_i - \widehat{m}(x, C_i)] + h^p \cdot \widehat{m}(x, C_i)\Bigg\}.
$$
:::

## Room for improvement

We have a doubly robust estimator of $\mu(x)$ for multiple continuous exposures. 

. . .

It requires nuisance estimation of $m(x,c)$ and $\pi(x \mid c)$. 

. . .

Fitting a $(p+q)$-regression and a $p$-variate conditional density with $q$ regressors can be hard! 

##

Can we find a low rank $Z$ that preserves all the information about $\mu(x)$ contained in $X$? 

# Sufficient dimension reduction

## Sufficient Dimension Reduction (SDR)

**Goal:** find a linear transformation $\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times d}$ that retains all information about $\mathbb{E}(Y\mid X)$ in ${X}$. 

$$
  \mathbb{E}(Y\mid {X}={x})
   = \mathbb{E}(Y\mid \beta^\top{X}=\beta^\top {x})
$$

We refer to $\text{span}(\beta)$ as the **central mean subspace (CMS)** denoted by $\mathcal{S}_{\mathbb{E}(Y\mid X)}$.

## Semiparametric Estimation of the CMS

The efficient score for $\beta$ (Luo and Cai, 2016) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
{U}(\beta)
&=
\frac{1}{\sigma^2(X)}
\left[
X -
\frac{\mathbb{E}\{X / \sigma^2(X) \mid \beta^\top X\}}
{\mathbb{E}\{1 / \sigma^2(X) \mid \beta^\top X\}}
\right] \\
&\qquad \times
\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}(Y\mid \beta^\top X)}{\partial (\beta^\top X)}
\big[Y - \mathbb{E}(Y \mid \beta^\top X)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Our estimate $\hat{\beta}$ solves $U(\hat{\beta})=0$ by estimating nuisances:

1. $\sigma^2(X):=\text{Var}(Y \mid X)$
2. $\mathbb{E}\{X / \sigma^2(X) \mid \beta^\top X\}$
3. $\mathbb{E}\{1 / \sigma^2(X) \mid \beta^\top X\}$
4. $\partial \mathbb{E}(Y\mid \beta^\top X) / \partial(\beta^\top X)$
5. $\mathbb{E}(Y\mid \beta^\top X)$

<!--
## Gradient-based Alternatives

A useful identity that leads to a computationally efficient alternative to $U(\beta)$ is:

::: callout-tip
## Key identity for the CMS

$$
  \frac{\partial \mathbb{E}(Y \mid {X}={x})}{\partial {x}}
  = \beta \frac{\partial \mathbb{E}(Y \mid \beta^\top {X}=\beta^\top {x})}{\partial (\beta^\top{x})}
  = \text{span}(\beta)
$$
:::

We can therefore estimate the CMS through the gradient rather than through several nuisance functions. 
-->

## Estimation with MAVE

The minimum average variance estimator (MAVE) from Xia et al. (2002) achieves estimation of CMS through a variation of local linear regression.

**Model:**
$$
  Y = g(\beta^\top X) + \varepsilon, 
  \qquad \mathbb{E}(\varepsilon \mid X) = 0
$$

**MAVE objective:**
\begin{align*}
  \min_{\beta^\top\beta = I_d,\; \{a_j,{b}_j\}}
  \sum_{j=1}^n \sum_{i=1}^n 
  \Big(Y_i - \{a_j +   {b}_j^\top \beta^\top ({X}_i - {X}_j)\}\Big)^2 w_{ij}
\end{align*}

where $w_{ij} = K_h(X_i-X_j) / \sum^n_{\ell=1} K_h(X_i - X_\ell)$. 

## Motivation for Causal SDR

Under confounding, it is clear that $\mathbb{E}(Y\mid X) \neq \mathbb{E}(Y^x)$. 

. . .

How can we bridge SDR for $\mathbb{E}(Y\mid X)$, with the doubly robust estimator $\hat{\mu}_{DR}(x)$ for the ERS from earlier? 

# Causal sufficient dimension reduction

## Model formulation

Suppose that there exists $\beta\in \mathbb{R}^{p \times d}$

$$
\mu(x) = \mu_\beta(\beta^\top x), \qquad x\in \mathcal{X}.
$$

For causal SDR, our new estimand of interest is the **causal central mean subspace** (CCMS) denoted as $\mathcal{S}_{\mathbb{E}(Y\mid X)}$. 

## Semiparametric Estimation in the Causal Setting

Using the theory of marginal structural models (Robins, 2000), Nabi et al. (2022) proposed the following locally efficient score for estimating $\mathcal{S}_{\mathbb{E}(Y^x)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{U}(\beta)
&=
\mathbb{E}_q\!\big[\mathbb{E}[{U}^*(\beta)\mid X,C]\mid C\big] \\
&\quad+
\frac{\pi^*(X)}{\pi(X\mid C)}
\Big({U}^*(\beta)-\mathbb{E}[{U}^*(\beta)\mid X,C]\Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $U^*$ is the MSM version of the association-based score $U(\beta)$ and $\mathbb{E}_q(\cdot)$ denotes the *causal* mean. 

## Total number of nuisance functions

The number of nuisances required to evaluate $\tilde{U}(\beta)$ is much larger.

1. $\text{Var}_q(Y \mid X)$
2. $m(x,c)$
3. $\pi(x\mid c)$
4. $\mathbb{E}_q[\cdot \mid C]$
5. $\mathbb{E}_q\{X / \sigma^2(X) \mid \beta^\top X\}$
6. $\mathbb{E}_q\{1 / \sigma^2(X) \mid \beta^\top X\}$
7. $\partial \mathbb{E}_q(Y\mid \beta^\top X) / \partial(\beta^\top X)$
8. $\mathbb{E}_q(Y\mid \beta^\top X)$

##

Is there a way to conduct causal SDR that avoids such heavy $\beta$-dependent nuisance estimation while attaining acceptable efficiency? 

## Multiple options for Causal SDR

::: {.callout-note icon="false" title="New strategy"}
Find new variables $\tilde{Y}$ and $\tilde{X}$ such that $\mathbb{E}(\tilde{Y} \mid \tilde{X}=x) = \mu_\beta(\beta^\top x)$. This allows us to take advantage of standard SDR methods. 
:::

. . .

```{=tex}
\begin{table}
\centering
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
Method & $Y$ variable & $X$ variable \\
\midrule
Oracle & $\mu(X_i)$ & $X_i$ \\
Regression adjustment (RA) & $\hat{\mu}_{RA}(X_i)$ & $X_i$ \\
Doubly robust (DR) & $\hat{\mu}_{DR}(X_i)$ & $X_i$ \\
Pseudo-outcome (PO) & $\hat{\xi}_i$ & $X_i$ \\
Residualized pair (RP) & $Y_i - \hat{\mathbb{E}}(Y \mid C_i)$ & $X - \hat{\mathbb{E}}(X \mid C_i)$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

## Structural dimension selection

**NW leave-one-out estimator**
$$
\hat{a}_{d0,j}
=
\frac{\sum_{i\neq j} K^{(i,j)}_{h_d} \tilde{Y_i}}
{\sum_{i\neq j} K^{(i,j)}_{h_d}},
K^{(i,j)}_{h_d}
=
K_{h_d}\{\hat{\beta}_1^\top(\tilde{X}_i-\tilde{X}_j),\dots,\hat{\beta}_d^\top(\tilde{X}_i-\tilde{X}_j)\}
$$

**Cross-validated error**
$$
\text{CV}_n(d)
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\big(\tilde{Y}_j-\hat{a}_{d0,j}\big)^2, \qquad \text{CV}_n(0)
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\tilde{Y}_i-\bar Y)^2
$$

**Estimated structural dimension**
$$
\hat d
=
\arg\min_{0\le d\le p}
\text{CV}_n(d)
$$

## Summary of causal SDR procedure

1. Estimate new response and exposure variables $(\tilde{Y}, \tilde{X})$
2. Input the new $(\tilde{Y},\tilde{X})$ into standard MAVE. 
3. Estimate structural dimension with $\hat{d}$. 
4. Apply Newton-Raphson update to estimate $\mathcal{S}_{\mathbb{E}(Y^x)}$. 

## Key question

What are the statistical properties of MAVE when 

1. Using noisy estimated versions of the true $Y$ and $X$?
2. When $\epsilon=0$ (since $\hat{\mu}(x)$ converges to a deterministic quantity)?  

## Causal MAVE convergence

::: {.callout-note icon="false" title="Theorem (Convergence of csMAVE)"}
Under regularity assumptions, if $h\to 0$, $d \geq d_0$, and $nh^p / \log(n)\to \infty$, then 
$$
\big\lVert (I_p-\hat{\beta}\hat{\beta}^\top) \beta\big\rVert = O_p(h^3 + h \delta_n + h^{-1}\delta_n^2+\textcolor{red}{h^{-1}R_n})
$$ 
where $\delta_n=\{\log n / (nh^p)\}^{1/2}$ and $\displaystyle \max_{1 \le i \le n} |\hat{\mu}(X_i) - \mu(X_i)| = O_p(R_n)$.
:::

The MAVE convergence rate is maintained so long as $R_n$ is fast enough (if $d_0$ is small and $m(x,c)$ and $\pi(x\mid c)$ are estimated well). 

## Dimension estimator

::: {.callout-note icon="false" title="Theorem (Consistency of $\\hat{d}$)"}
The estimator $\hat{d}$ converges to the structural dimension $d_0$ in probability as $n\to\infty$.
:::

## Post dimension reduction inference

::: {.callout-note icon="false" title="Theorem (Post dimension reduction inference)"}
Let $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\beta}_n - \beta_0) = O_P(1)$ and $\hat{\mu}_{DR}(x;\beta)$ be the DR estimator using the intervention $\beta^{\top} X$. Under assumptions, if $h\to 0$ and $nh^d \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$, then $\hat{\mu}_{DR}({x}_0; \hat{\beta}_n)$ consistently estimates $\mu({x}_0)$. The estimator based on the estimated reduction is asymptotically equivalent to the oracle estimator:
$$
\sqrt{nh_n^d} \big\{ \hat{\mu}_{DR}({x}_0; \hat{\beta}_n) - \hat{\mu}_{DR}({x}_0; \beta_0) \big\} = o_P(1)
$$
:::

Plugging in $\hat{\beta}$ into the DR estimator gives no penalty (asymptotically) compared to the true $\beta_0$. 

## Why not interpret $\beta$ directly?

Suppose $d_0=2$.  

### Basis representation 1

$$
\beta =
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 \\
0 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Naive interpretation:

- $X_1$ important
- $X_2$ important
- $X_3$ unimportant

---

### Basis representation 2 (same subspace)

$$
\tilde{\beta} =
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
0 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Columns remain **orthonormal**, and

$$
\text{span}(\beta) = \text{span}(\tilde{\beta})
$$

But the entries suggest **different importance** for $X_1$ and $X_2$.

---

### Key point

The individual coefficients of $\beta$ **depend on the chosen orthonormal basis**.

Instead we define the **Subspace Importance Score**

## Subspace Importance Score

We define a measure of variable importance called the **Subspace Importance Score (SIS)**.

Let
$$
P = \beta \beta^\top
$$

be the **projection matrix onto the central subspace** $\text{span}(\beta)$.

For exposure $X_j$, define

$$
\lambda_j = P_{jj}.
$$

---

### Properties

- For each $j=1,\dots,p$,  
  $$
  0 \le \lambda_j \le 1.
  $$

- The scores sum to the **structural dimension**:
  $$
  \sum_{j=1}^p \lambda_j = d.
  $$

- $\lambda_j$ depends only on the **subspace $\text{span}(\beta)$**, and is invariant to the particular basis used for $\beta$.

---

### Interpretation

$\lambda_j$ measures **how strongly exposure $X_j$ contributes to the CCMS**.

- $\lambda_j=1$ → $X_j$ is completely present in the true ERS  
- $\lambda_j=0$ → $X_j$ has no role in the true ERS

# Simulation Analysis

## Simulation Setup (Part 1)

**Data Generating Process**

- **Exposures & Confounders:** $p=10$ exposures ($X$) with AR(1) correlation ($\rho=0.8$) and $q=5$ independent normal confounders ($C$). 
- **True Subspace:** The structural dimension is $d_0=2$, with $\beta$ solely spanning $X_1$ and $X_2$.
- **Outcome Model:** $Y = h(Z) + g(C) + f(Z,C) + \epsilon$
  - $h(Z) = 4\sin(Z_1) + 2Z_2^2 + Z_1Z_2$.
  - $g(C) = 5.0 \tanh(C_1) + 5.0 (C_2^2 - 1) + 5.0 (C_3^2 - 1) + 5.0 \sin(1.5 C_4)$
  - $f(Z,C)=5C_5(Z_1 + Z_2)$

## Simulation Setup (Part 2)

**Nuisance Function Estimation**

- **$\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X, C]$:** 2-layer neural network via `torch` using 150 epochs, a 0.05 learning rate, and SiLU activation functions.
- **$\pi(x \mid c)$:** Linear multivariate normal (MVN) density estimation.
- **$\mathbb{E}[Y \mid C], \mathbb{E}[X \mid C]$:** `SuperLearner` ensemble combining GLM, Lasso (`glmnet`), and Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines (`earth`).

## Comparison methods

\begin{table}
\centering
\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tabular}{>{\bfseries}l p{8.5cm}}
\toprule
Method & \textbf{Estimation of the Central Mean Subspace ($\beta$)} \\
\midrule
PCA & Top eigenvectors of $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X})$ (unsupervised). \\
pCCA & Maximizes linear correlation between $\mathbf{X}$ and $Y$ after linearly partialling out $\mathbf{C}$. \\
MAVE & MAVE applied to $(Y, X)$. \\
\textcolor{red}{Oracle-MAVE} & MAVE applied to $\big(\mu(X), X\big)$. \\ 
\textcolor{red}{RA-MAVE}  & MAVE applied to $(\hat{\mu}_{RA}({X}), X)$. \\
\textcolor{red}{DR-MAVE} & MAVE applied to $(\hat{\mu}_{DR}({X}), X)$ \\
\textcolor{red}{PO-MAVE}  & MAVE applied to $(\hat{\xi}, X)$ \\
\textcolor{red}{RP-MAVE} & MAVE applied to  $(Y - \hat{\mathbb{E}}[Y\mid C], {X} - \hat{\mathbb{E}}[{X}\mid {C}])$. \\
Full X & No dimension reduction. $\hat{\beta}=I_p$. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
}
\end{table}

## Evaluation metrics

We want to know how well we estimate

1. $\mathcal{S}_{\mathbb{E}(Y^x)}$: the causal central mean subspace
2. $d_0$: the structural dimension
3. $\mu_\beta(z)$: the causal ERS, using $\hat{Z}=\hat{\beta}^\top X$ in place of $X$.

# Results

## Estimation of $\mathcal{S}_{Y^x}$

```{=tex}
\begin{table}
\centering
\caption{Mean (SD) Frobenius norm error between estimated and true $\mathcal{S}_{Y^x}$ when $d_0$ is known.}

\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tabular}{lccccc}
\toprule
Method & $n=100$ & $n=500$ & $n=1000$ & $n=2500$ & $n=5000$ \\
\midrule
PCA         & 1.997 (0.002) & \textbf{1.997 (0.001)} & 1.997 (0.001) & 1.997 (0.000) & \textbf{1.997 (0.000)} \\
pCCA        & 1.316 (0.119) & \textbf{1.202 (0.177)} & 1.100 (0.202) & 0.925 (0.219) & \textbf{0.743 (0.203)} \\
MAVE        & 1.468 (0.220) & \textbf{1.247 (0.247)} & 1.239 (0.229) & 1.281 (0.179) & \textbf{1.299 (0.148)} \\
Oracle-MAVE & 0.203 (0.084) & \textbf{0.030 (0.010)} & 0.015 (0.005) & 0.006 (0.002) & \textbf{0.003 (0.001)} \\
RA-MAVE     & 1.276 (0.331) & \textbf{0.442 (0.388)} & 0.216 (0.272) & 0.072 (0.152) & \textbf{0.026 (0.027)} \\
DR-MAVE     & 1.458 (0.253) & \textbf{0.546 (0.352)} & 0.297 (0.245) & 0.137 (0.138) & \textbf{0.079 (0.066)} \\
PO-MAVE     & 1.486 (0.315) & \textbf{0.691 (0.484)} & 0.450 (0.433) & 0.292 (0.368) & \textbf{0.230 (0.317)} \\
RP-MAVE     & 1.658 (0.173) & \textbf{1.339 (0.220)} & 1.171 (0.263) & 0.907 (0.332) & \textbf{0.689 (0.340)} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
}

\end{table}
```
## Order Determination

```{=tex}
\scriptsize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
```
```{=tex}
\begin{table}
\centering
\caption{Proportion of simulations selecting each structural dimension.}

\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tabular}{llcccccc}
\toprule
$n$ & Method & $d=0$ & $d=1$ & $d=2$ & $d=3$ & $d=4$ & $d=5$  \\
\midrule

500 & MAVE        & 0.00 & 0.00 & \textbf{0.11} & 0.75 & 0.14 & 0.00 \\
    & Oracle-MAVE & 0.00 & 0.00 & \textbf{1.00} & 0.00 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\
    & RA-MAVE     & 0.01 & 0.03 & \textbf{0.88} & 0.07 & 0.01 & 0.00 \\
    & DR-MAVE     & 0.01 & 0.05 & \textbf{0.85} & 0.07 & 0.01 & 0.00 \\
    & PO-MAVE     & 0.03 & 0.06 & \textbf{0.73} & 0.14 & 0.03 & 0.00 \\
    & RP-MAVE     & 0.00 & 0.28 & \textbf{0.29} & 0.23 & 0.15 & 0.04 \\

\midrule

5000 & MAVE        & 0.00 & 0.00 & \textbf{0.00} & 1.00 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\
     & Oracle-MAVE & 0.00 & 0.00 & \textbf{1.00} & 0.00 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\
     & RA-MAVE     & 0.00 & 0.00 & \textbf{1.00} & 0.00 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\
     & DR-MAVE     & 0.00 & 0.00 & \textbf{1.00} & 0.00 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\
     & PO-MAVE     & 0.01 & 0.00 & \textbf{0.92} & 0.07 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\
     & RP-MAVE     & 0.00 & 0.21 & \textbf{0.74} & 0.05 & 0.00 & 0.00 \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
}

\end{table}
```
## Subspace Importance Scores ($n=500$)

![](../results/jasa-initial-submission/ATL-AA/diag_plot_n=500.pdf)

## Subspace Importance Scores ($n=5000$)

![](../results/jasa-initial-submission/ATL-AA/diag_plot_n=5000.pdf)

## ERS Pointwise Bias

![Bias of ERS estimation.](../results/jasa-initial-submission/final_results_nnet/additive/plot_pw_bias.pdf)

## ERS Pointwise RMSE

![RMSE of ERS estimation.](../results/jasa-initial-submission/final_results_nnet/additive/plot_pw_rmse.pdf)

## ERS Pointwise Coverage

![Coverage of 95% pointwise CIs.](../results/jasa-initial-submission/final_results_nnet/additive/plot_pw_coverage.pdf)

# Real data analysis

## Motivating Dataset

**Study population:** $N = 303$ African-American women in Atlanta from 2014-2020 with term births. 

**Exposure:** Four log2-transformed PFAS exposures ($p$=4): PFOS, PFOA, PFNA, PFHxS. 

**Covariates:** Maternal age, BMI, education, tobacco use, marijuana use, and infant sex.

**Outcome:** Birthweight in grams. 

## Subspace Importance Scores

![Subspace importance scores between causal SDR and assocation-based SDR](../results/jasa-initial-submission/ATL-AA/diag_plot_notitle.pdf)

## Estimate of the ERS

![Causal ERS estimate with 95% CIs.](../results/jasa-initial-submission/ATL-AA/causal_ers_no_title.pdf){width="90%"}

## Summary of causal SDR

We have designed a method that:

1. Implements dimension reduction for $X$ that preserves the causal dose-response (Theorem 1)

. . .

2. Consistently estimates the true dimension (Theorem 2)

. . .

3. Improves efficiency and coverage over using the original $X$ and incurs no asymptotic plug-in bias for ERS estimation (Theorem 3)

. . .

4. Leverages existing off-the-shelf tools for SDR

. . .

5. Gives clear outputs for causal ERS estimation and variable importance


## Acknowledgments

Thank you to my postdoc advisers Drs. Razieh Nabi and Howard Chang, and collaborators Drs. Donghai Liang and Anne Dunlop for access to the ATL-AA cohort data!

# Questions?

# Appendix

## Causal Identification Assumptions

1.  **Consistency**: If $X=x$, then the observed outcome equals the potential outcome: $Y = Y^x$

2.  **Positivity**: Every exposure level has nonzero probability (given covariates): $\pi(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{c}) \geq \varepsilon > 0$

3.  **Conditional ignorability**: Given covariates, exposure is independent of potential outcomes: $Y^{\mathbf{x}} \;\perp\!\!\!\perp\; \mathbf{X}\mid \mathbf{C}$

::: {.callout-warning icon="false" title="Remark (Positivity)"}
We make the positivity assumption here for identification purposes rather than assuming its validity in environmental applications. However, identifying a low rank $Z$ can make positivity more tenable than for $X$. 
:::

## Causal response variables

Recall again the underlying model:

$$
\mu(x)=g(\beta^\top x), \qquad x\in\mathcal{X}.
$$

Notice that if $\mathbb{E}(W \mid X=x)=\mu(x)$, then plugging $(W,X)$ into the MAVE yields a valid estimate of the CCMS. 

## RA and DR responses

One approach is to estimate $\mu(x)$ at $X_1,X_2,...,X_n$ to yield $\hat{\mu}(X_1),...,\hat{\mu}(X_n)$.

This can be achieved with the mentioned RA and DR approaches.

## Pseudo-outcomes (PO)

By a multivariate extension of Kennedy et al. (2017), a causal pseudo-outcome can be desisgned:

$$
\xi_{j}=\frac{Y_{j}-m(X_{j},C_{j})}{\pi(X_{j}\mid C_{j})} \left\{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \pi(X_{j}\mid C_{i}) \right\} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n m(X_{j}, C_{i}) 
$$
which satisfies 

$$
\xi = \mu_\beta(\beta^\top X) + \epsilon_\xi, \qquad \mathbb{E}(\epsilon_\xi \mid X) = 0.
$$

## Residualized Pair (RP)

The RA, DR, and PO approaches all require estimation of $m(x,c)$ and $\pi(x\mid c)$, which may be unappealing if the goal is dimension reduction. 

If we are willing to assume no interaction between $X$ and $C$ in $m(x,c)$:

::: {.callout-note icon="false" title="Proposition"}
Let $\tilde{Y}=Y-\mathbb{E}(Y\mid C)$ and $\tilde{X}=X-\mathbb{E}(X\mid C)$. Under additive confounding, for some smooth function $\tilde{g}$,

$$
    \mathbb{E}(\tilde{Y} \mid \tilde{X}) = \tilde{g}(\beta^\top \tilde{X}).
$$
:::

## Comparison between MAVE and EE estimators

```{=tex}
\scriptsize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
```
```{=tex}
\begin{table}
\centering
\caption{Mean (SD) of Frobenius norm error between the estimated and true subspace between MAVE and one-step EE.}

\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tabular}{lcccccccccc}
\toprule
& \multicolumn{2}{c}{$n=100$} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$n=500$} &
\multicolumn{2}{c}{$n=1000$} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$n=2500$} &
\multicolumn{2}{c}{$n=5000$} \\
\cmidrule(lr){2-3}\cmidrule(lr){4-5}\cmidrule(lr){6-7}\cmidrule(lr){8-9}\cmidrule(lr){10-11}
Method & MAVE & EE & MAVE & EE & MAVE & EE & MAVE & EE & MAVE & EE \\
\midrule
Oracle & 0.216 (0.136) & 0.635 (0.261) & 0.030 (0.010) & 0.379 (0.131) & 0.015 (0.005) & 0.316 (0.107) & 0.006 (0.002) & 0.254 (0.086) & 0.003 (0.001) & 0.219 (0.070) \\
RA     & 1.343 (0.341) & 1.352 (0.332) & 0.447 (0.392) & 0.463 (0.384) & 0.223 (0.288) & 0.232 (0.282) & 0.073 (0.157) & 0.080 (0.153) & 0.026 (0.035) & 0.031 (0.035) \\
DR     & 1.537 (0.267) & 1.543 (0.262) & 0.568 (0.369) & 0.589 (0.361) & 0.303 (0.257) & 0.323 (0.253) & 0.140 (0.151) & 0.162 (0.148) & 0.078 (0.058) & 0.100 (0.061) \\
PO     & 1.487 (0.318) & 1.491 (0.315) & 0.663 (0.450) & 0.657 (0.445) & 0.432 (0.401) & 0.412 (0.393) & 0.287 (0.345) & 0.246 (0.333) & 0.221 (0.293) & 0.165 (0.280) \\
RP     & 1.768 (0.218) & 1.776 (0.217) & 1.413 (0.259) & 1.443 (0.254) & 1.192 (0.240) & 1.226 (0.237) & 0.913 (0.255) & 0.952 (0.249) & 0.676 (0.285) & 0.697 (0.271) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
}

\end{table}
```
## Assumption for csMAVE

::: {.callout-warning icon="false" title="Assumption (csMAVE)"}
\small

1.  $(Y_i,X_i,C_i)$ are i.i.d.

2.  $X$ and $\mu(X)$ have finite moments of all orders.

3.  $f_X(x)$ has bounded fourth derivatives and is bounded away from zero on compact support $\mathcal{X}$.

4.  $\mu(X)=g(\beta^\top X)$ with $\beta^\top\beta=I_d$ and $g$ having bounded continuous third derivatives.

5.  $K(\cdot)$ is bounded, spherically symmetric, differentiable, and satisfies $\int UU^\top K(U)\,dU = I$.

6.  $\displaystyle \max_{1 \le i \le n} |\hat{\mu}(X_i) - \mu(X_i)| = O_p(R_n)$.
:::
